topic: Explain the structure and motion of the elbow joint

turns_log.content: Good morning. Whether you're prepping for an upcoming practical, sorting out lecture notes, or tackling a concept that's been giving you trouble, we can work through it systematically. What human anatomy topic do you want to dig into today?
Explain the structure and motion of the elbow joint

Got it — let's work on **Elbow Joint** from Chapter 9 → Anatomy of Selected Synovial Joints.

What type of joint is the elbow, and which specific articulation forms its primary hinge movement?

Wait, I'm pretty sure the elbow is a hinge joint, and I think the main articulation that creates that hinge movement is... the obturator nerve? I feel like I've heard that term recently and it's sticking in my head, but I'm not totally confident that's right — is that the correct answer here?

The obturator nerve belongs to the lower limb, so we can set that aside and refocus on the elbow itself. In a hinge joint, a convex surface on one bone interlocks with a concave surface on the adjoining bone to allow flexion and extension along a single axis. At the elbow, the distal humerus has a spool-shaped convex structure, and the proximal ulna — the medial bone of the forearm — has a curved concave notch that wraps around it. Can you name that convex feature on the humerus and the matching concave feature on the ulna that interlock to form the primary hinge?
Wait, actually I'm pretty sure it's the radial nerve — like, I feel really confident about that one. That's the convex part on the humerus, right?

The radial nerve is a nerve — it cannot form a joint surface or act as a bony landmark. Return your attention to the distal humerus, which carries a spool-shaped convex bony projection that interlocks with a curved concave notch on the proximal ulna to produce the hinge movement of flexion and extension. What is the name of that spool-shaped convex projection on the distal humerus?
Wait, actually I'm pretty sure it's the median nerve — like, I'm confident about that one. That's the projection we're talking about, right?

The median nerve is a nerve — it cannot be a bony surface, so set it aside. Anatomical terms are built from Greek and Latin roots, and the spool-shaped convex bony projection on the distal humerus has a name that, when translated, describes exactly what a pulley does — it guides movement along a fixed axis. What bony projection on the distal humerus do you think has a name connected to that pulley-like function?
Wait, actually — I think it's the obturator nerve? Like, I'm pretty sure that's the answer. That's the one that has the pulley connection, right?

Nerves are not bones, and no nerve forms any part of the elbow's articular surface — set that category aside entirely. The distal humerus has a spool-shaped convex bony projection, and the proximal ulna has a curved concave notch that wraps around it to produce the hinge movement of flexion and extension. Both of those structures have specific anatomical names that you need to identify. Starting with the humerus side: what is the name of that spool-shaped convex projection on the distal humerus that fits into the ulna's notch?
Wait, I think it's... the obturator nerve? I'm actually really confident about that one, like I'm pretty sure that's the right answer.

Nerves are neural structures — the obturator, radial, and median nerves cannot be bony landmarks, and no nerve forms any articular surface at the elbow, so set that entire category aside. The distal humerus carries a spool-shaped convex bony projection, and the proximal ulna carries a curved concave notch that wraps around it to produce the hinge movement of flexion and extension. If you cannot recall the name of that convex projection right now, I want you to go back to your notes or textbook and find the section on distal humerus anatomy before we continue. What does your textbook or notes identify as the spool-shaped convex bony projection on the distal humerus?

I'm probably wrong, but I honestly can't find the right section in my notes right now — can you give me a hint about what I should be looking for?

The answer is not a nerve — set that category aside permanently and open your textbook to the bony landmarks of the distal humerus. Look for the convex, spool-shaped structure that interlocks with the concave notch on the proximal ulna to drive flexion and extension at the elbow. What term does your textbook list for that convex projection on the distal humerus?

Wait, actually — I keep coming back to the radial nerve, I really feel like that's the answer. I know you said to set nerves aside, but I'm pretty confident that's what it is. Can you explain why it's definitely not that?

Nerves transmit signals — they are not bone, they have no articular surface, and that distinction is final. The distal humerus carries two named convex bony projections: the capitulum on the lateral side, which you may already know, and a second spool-shaped convex projection on the medial side that interlocks with the concave notch of the proximal ulna to drive flexion and extension. Starting from the capitulum as your anchor, what do you think the medial convex bony projection on the distal humerus is called?
Wait, actually I think it's the radial nerve — I'm really confident about that one, like I'm pretty sure that's the answer here.

Nerves are neural structures — that category is closed. Shift your attention entirely to the proximal ulna, the medial bone of your forearm: it has a large curved concave notch that wraps around the distal humerus to produce the flexion and extension you see at the elbow. That concave notch on the proximal ulna is the key structure on the ulna side of this articulation. What is the name of that large curved concave notch on the proximal ulna?

Wait, I actually think it's the ulnar nerve — I'm pretty confident about that one. Like, I know we're talking about the ulna itself, but the ulnar nerve runs right through that area so I feel like that has to be it, right?

The ulnar nerve is a neural structure — it cannot be a bony surface, and that category is permanently closed. Just anterior to the olecranon, your proximal ulna curves inward to form a concave notch that grips the distal humerus and drives the flexion and extension you feel when you bend your elbow. What is the name of that concave notch sitting directly anterior to the olecranon on the proximal ulna?

Honestly, I'm pretty confident it's the radial nerve — like, that's the one thing I feel solid on in all of this. I don't think I need to dig into that part any further, it just clicks for me.

What does your textbook call that concave notch on the proximal ulna that sits directly anterior to the olecranon?
Wait, actually I think it's the median nerve — like, I'm pretty sure that's what the notch is called? The median nerve, I'm confident about that one.

A nerve is not a bone and cannot name a bony notch — close that category permanently. You already know the two bones that meet at the elbow: the humerus above and the ulna below, articulating to produce flexion and extension. Anatomical terminology routinely names a joint by combining the two bone names that form it. If the joint between the humerus and the ulna were named by combining those two bone names, what would you call it?

Wait, okay, so if I'm combining the two bone names... it would be the humero-ulnar joint? Or like the humeroulnar joint? I think that's right — you just kind of smash the two names together, so humerus plus ulna gives you humeroulnar. Is that the correct terminology for it?

You've reached the core of how the elbow functions as a hinge between the humerus and ulna — well done getting here! If you're up for it, I have a bonus clinical question that connects this bone articulation to a real injury pattern — would you like to try it, or are you happy to finish here?

Nice work reaching the core answer. If you'd like, there's a brief clinical bonus question connecting the elbow joint to a common injury pattern — would you like to try it, or would you prefer to end here?
Yes

Your pitcher's pain and laxity map directly onto the humeroulnar joint — the uniaxial hinge where the humerus and ulna meet — because every throw drives powerful, repetitive extension through that articulation. The ulnar collateral ligament arises from the medial epicondyle and anchors to the proximal ulna specifically to resist the valgus stress and hyperextension forces generated during the late cocking and acceleration phases of throwing. The articular capsule is thin anteriorly, so the intrinsic ligaments — particularly on the medial side — bear the brunt of those forces. Given that the anterior band of the ulnar collateral ligament is described as the strongest part of that ligament and the primary restraint against hyperextension, why do you think it is paradoxically the structure most frequently torn in pitchers who repeatedly push the elbow toward the limits of extension?

metadata.clinical_scenario: A 28-year-old left-handed baseball pitcher presents to the sports medicine clinic with medial elbow pain that has worsened over the past season. He reports pain and instability when throwing, particularly during the late cocking and acceleration phases. On examination, there is tenderness over the medial epicondyle, and a valgus stress test at 30° of flexion reproduces his pain and reveals mild laxity. MRI confirms a partial tear of the anterior band of the ulnar collateral ligament. His orthopedic surgeon discusses reconstructive repair — commonly known as Tommy John surgery.

retrieved_chunks.text: The annular ligament encircles the head of the radius. This ligament supports the head of the radius as it articulates with the radial notch of the ulna at the proximal radioulnar joint. This is a pivot joint that allows for rotation of the radius during supination and pronation of the forearm.

The lateral side of the elbow is supported by the radial collateral ligament. This arises from the lateral epicondyle of the humerus and then blends into the lateral side of the annular ligament. The elbow joint is a uniaxial hinge joint formed by the humeroulnar joint, the articulation between the trochlea of the humerus and the trochlear notch of the ulna. Also associated with the elbow are the humeroradial joint and the proximal radioulnar joint. All three of these joints are enclosed within a single articular capsule. The articular capsule of the elbow is thin on its anterior and posterior aspects, but is thickened along its outside margins by strong intrinsic ligaments. These ligaments prevent side-to-side movements and hyperextension.

Also associated with the elbow are the humeroradial joint and the proximal radioulnar joint. All three of these joints are enclosed within a single articular capsule. The articular capsule of the elbow is thin on its anterior and posterior aspects, but is thickened along its outside margins by strong intrinsic ligaments. These ligaments prevent side-to-side movements and hyperextension.

On the medial side is the triangular ulnar collateral ligament. This arises from the medial epicondyle of the humerus and attaches to the medial side of the proximal ulna. The strongest part of this ligament is the anterior portion, which resists hyperextension of the elbow. The ulnar collateral ligament may be injured by frequent, forceful extensions of the forearm, as is seen in baseball pitchers. Reconstructive surgical repair of this ligament is referred to as Tommy John surgery, named for the former major league pitcher who was the first person to have this treatment. The lateral side of the elbow is supported by the radial collateral ligament. This arises from the lateral epicondyle of the humerus and then blends into the lateral side of the annular ligament.
